---
title: "Data and methods"
subtitle: "Green infrastructure and affordable housing data behind the dashboard"
toc: true
toc-depth: 2
---

This page summarizes how each dataset was processed and what each dashboard
variable means. The visual overview below is a quick reference; the full
documentation follows it and is maintained as `references/data_and_methods.md`
in the repository.

Everything here is cross-sectional and descriptive. The measures show where
affordable housing and environmental conditions co-locate across census units.
They do not, on their own, establish a disparity between housing types or a
causal effect, because the data currently contain no comparison housing group.

## Data streams at a glance

| Stream | Source | Native resolution | Vintage | Dashboard variables |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Tree canopy and land cover | Chesapeake Bay LULC, 2022 Edition | 1 m | ~2017 (DC), ~2018 (MD) | `canopy_pct`, `natural_canopy_pct` |
| Surface temperature and spectral indices | Landsat 8 and 9, Collection 2 Level-2 | 30 m (LST ~100 m effective) | Summer 2021 to 2023 | `mean_lst`, `mean_ndvi`, `mean_ndbi` |
| LIHTC affordable housing | HUD LIHTC database (LIHTCPUB) | point | Current inventory | `lihtc_units_low_income`, `lihtc_units_total`, `lihtc_projects` |

## Units of analysis

All variables are aggregated to U.S. Census areal units (2022 TIGER/Line). Two
geographies run in parallel so results can be compared across scale.

| Geography | Units (MD + DC) | Units with at least one LIHTC project | Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| Census block group | 4,650 | 741 | Default; finest unit carrying ACS data |
| Census tract | 1,681 | 545 | Coarser scale for sensitivity checks |

## Processing pipelines

How the three raw sources become the unit-level table the dashboard reads.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  C["Chesapeake 1 m<br/>land cover"] --> Z["Zonal aggregation<br/>to census units"]
  L["Landsat 8 and 9<br/>summer composite"] --> Z
  H["HUD LIHTC<br/>inventory"] --> J["Spatial join<br/>to census units"]
  Z --> T["Unit-level<br/>analysis table"]
  J --> T
  T --> D["Dashboard<br/>bivariate map"]
```

### Tree canopy

Canopy combines the four canopy classes and is divided by classified land, so
open water and unclassified area (including military installations masked to
nodata) do not distort the share.

```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
  A["Chesapeake 1 m<br/>12-class land cover"] --> B["exactextract<br/>area-weighted class fractions"]
  B --> C["Canopy = classes<br/>3, 10, 11, 12"]
  B --> D["Land = classified area<br/>minus water"]
  C --> E["canopy_pct =<br/>canopy / land x 100"]
  D --> E
  E --> F{"Classified land<br/>vs Census land"}
  F -->|gap small| G["canopy_reliable = true"]
  F -->|gap large| H["canopy set to missing<br/>open water, military nodata"]
```

### Surface temperature and indices

Indices are computed for each scene, then reduced to a per-pixel median across
three summers. This order matters: the median is taken over the indices, not over
a composite of raw bands.

```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
  A["Landsat 8 and 9<br/>Collection 2 Level-2"] --> B["Filter: summer 2021 to 2023<br/>scene cloud below 60 percent"]
  B --> C["Mask cloud, shadow,<br/>cirrus, saturated pixels"]
  C --> D["Apply scale factors<br/>reflectance and temperature"]
  D --> E["Per scene: LST, NDVI,<br/>NDBI, NDWI, albedo"]
  E --> F["Median across<br/>3 summers, 30 m"]
  F --> G["Zonal mean per unit<br/>mean_lst, mean_ndvi, ..."]
```

### LIHTC affordable housing

```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
  A["HUD national LIHTC<br/>LIHTCPUB"] --> B["Restrict to MD and DC<br/>1,216 projects"]
  B --> C["Geocode; drop 77 without<br/>coordinates, 3 outside layer"]
  C --> D["Spatial join<br/>project point in census unit"]
  D --> E["Aggregate per unit<br/>low-income units, total units, projects"]
```

## Reading the bivariate grid

The dashboard sorts units into three classes on each axis and crosses them into a
3 by 3 grid. The corner cell marks co-location: the units with the most LIHTC and
the worst environmental value. Its position moves with the variable, since a low
value is worse for canopy but a high value is worse for temperature.

```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
  subgraph grid["3 x 3 bivariate classes"]
    direction TB
    R2["environment: high"]
    R1["environment: mid"]
    R0["environment: low"]
  end
  note["Corner cell = most LIHTC units<br/>+ worst environment.<br/>Low canopy, or high temperature."]
  R0 -.-> note
```

For canopy the worst class is low, for surface temperature it is high, so the
highlighted corner flips accordingly. The correlation reported in the dashboard is
an unadjusted rank correlation across units, shown without a significance marker
because at these sample sizes significance is uninformative.

# Housing type and environment (parcel-scale dashboard)

The bivariate dashboard above shows where LIHTC affordable housing and environmental
conditions co-locate across census units. It cannot compare affordable housing to
anything, because the areal data carry no other housing group. A second, parcel-scale
dashboard adds that comparison. It works at the **building** scale and sorts
multifamily rental stock into three types — subsidized, NOAH, and market-rate — then
contrasts them on the canopy, summer heat, and flood exposure measured at each
building's own footprint.

Everything here remains cross-sectional and descriptive. A difference between NOAH
and market-rate buildings reflects where the two types sit, not an effect of the
housing type. Siting, building age, and neighborhood composition differ across the
types and none is adjusted for, so a gap is a statement about co-location, not a
disparity that housing type produced.

## What the three types mean

None of these categories comes from a registry. Market-rate is the residual, and
NOAH is derived from an affordability proxy, so the definitions are choices that a
reader should be able to see and contest.

| Type | Definition | Buildings |
|---|---|---|
| **Subsidized** | Footprint within 30 m of an active LIHTC or NHPD record | 865 |
| **NOAH** | Unsubsidized, assessed value/unit at or below the 60%-AMI cutoff | 2,639 |
| **Market-rate** | Unsubsidized, assessed value/unit above the cutoff | 1,512 |
| **Unknown** | Unsubsidized, no trustworthy unit count | 348 |

NOAH — naturally occurring affordable housing — is the policy-relevant category:
unsubsidized stock that rents affordably without a program and can be lost to the
market at any time. It has no subsidy record, so it is inferred from an affordability
threshold on the assessor's **value per unit**, anchored to Area Median Income (see
below), using public data only.

"Multifamily" here means purpose-built and small **rental** apartment stock.
Condominium and cooperative parcels are excluded: they are owner-occupied tenure,
recorded one parcel per unit rather than per building, so their per-unit values
would not be comparable and would swamp the apartment stock by count. One
consequence to state plainly — investor-owned condominium units that are in fact
rented out are missed, because the assessor data cannot distinguish an owner-occupied
condo from a rented one without an ownership-occupancy join. The rental universe is
therefore the apartment stock, not every unit that happens to be rented.

## Housing-type data streams

| Stream | Source | Native resolution | Vintage | Dashboard variables |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Parcels and assessed values | MD MdProperty View; DC Owner Polygons | parcel polygon | Feb 2026 (MD); 2026 snapshot (DC) | `value_per_unit`, `units`, `year_built` |
| Subsidized housing | HUD LIHTC (LIHTCPUB); NHPD national extract | point | Current inventory | `housing_type = subsidized` |
| Tree canopy and land cover | Chesapeake Bay LULC, 2022 Edition | 1 m | ~2017 (DC), ~2018 (MD) | `canopy_pct`, `natural_canopy_pct` |
| Surface temperature and indices | Landsat 8 and 9, Collection 2 Level-2 | 30 m (LST ~100 m effective) | Summer 2021 to 2023 | `mean_lst`, `mean_ndvi` |
| Flood exposure | FEMA National Flood Hazard Layer | polygon | current NFHL | `in_floodplain` (activates when the layer is supplied) |

## Unit of analysis: the building

The unit is the **multifamily building**, not the parcel and not the census area.

| | Parcels | Buildings | Multi-parcel buildings |
|---|---|---|---|
| MD (6 counties) + DC | 11,089 | 5,364 | 1,636 |

A single apartment complex is frequently split across several assessor parcels: one
parcel carries the value and units, and contiguous sub-parcels (garages, common
areas, phased buildings) report one unit or none. Left as parcels, roughly 2,200 of
the 11,089 report exactly one unit while carrying large values, which makes value per
unit meaningless. Aggregating to the building is the prerequisite for every
comparison the dashboard makes.

## Housing-type processing pipeline

How assessor parcels become the typed, environmentally-joined building table the
dashboard reads.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  MD["MD MdProperty View<br/>parcels + assessed values"] --> P["Multifamily<br/>parcels (11,089)"]
  DC["DC Owner Polygons<br/>parcels + assessed values"] --> P
  P --> B["Dissolve to buildings<br/>by contiguity (5,364)"]
  B --> S["Flag subsidized<br/>LIHTC + NHPD within 30 m"]
  S --> N["Split unsubsidized by<br/>value/unit -> NOAH vs market"]
  N --> E["Extract canopy, LST, flood<br/>per footprint"]
  E --> D["Housing-type<br/>dashboard"]
```

### Building aggregation

Parcels that share a boundary within the same jurisdiction are treated as one
building; their values, units, and areas are summed. Owner is deliberately not used
to merge, because the only owner field available (a mailing address in Maryland, an
owner name in DC) is shared across unrelated properties held by the same management
company and would merge buildings that are not one structure.

```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
  A["Multifamily parcels<br/>11,089"] --> B["Queen contiguity<br/>within jurisdiction"]
  B --> C["Connected components<br/>= building groups"]
  C --> D["Sum value, units, area;<br/>max year built; mode use"]
  D --> E["5,364 buildings"]
  E --> F{"value/unit<br/>> $2,000,000?"}
  F -->|yes| G["Unit count untrustworthy<br/>-> type = unknown"]
  F -->|no| H["value/unit is<br/>the affordability axis"]
```

The value-per-unit ceiling catches buildings whose value and unit count sit on
separate parcels that did not merge, leaving an impossible per-unit figure. These
are set aside as `unknown` rather than counted as expensive market-rate stock.

### Subsidy flag

A building is subsidized if its footprint is near an active LIHTC or NHPD record.
NHPD is filtered to active subsidies: a property whose subsidy has expired is no
longer subsidized, and belongs back in the unsubsidized pool.

```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
  A["HUD LIHTC<br/>MD + DC points"] --> C["Union of<br/>subsidy points"]
  B["NHPD national extract<br/>MD + DC, status = active"] --> C
  C --> D["Building footprint<br/>within 30 m?"]
  D -->|yes| E["subsidized"]
  D -->|no| F["unsubsidized<br/>-> NOAH / market split"]
```

### NOAH versus market-rate: an AMI-anchored cutoff

Among unsubsidized buildings, an affordability cutoff on assessed value per unit
separates NOAH from market-rate. The cutoff is not an arbitrary quantile of the
value distribution; it is anchored to an explicit affordability standard — a
household at a target Area Median Income (AMI) level — and every step of the
translation from that standard to a dollar figure is either sourced or measured.

```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
  A["HUD 80% AMI income limit<br/>3-person, FY2024"] --> B["Scale to target level<br/>(50 / 60 / 80% AMI)"]
  B --> C["Affordable rent =<br/>30% of income / 12"]
  C --> D["Affordable market value/unit =<br/>annual rent x GRM (10x)"]
  D --> E["Affordable assessed value/unit =<br/>market value x assessment ratio"]
  E --> F{"building value/unit<br/>at or below cutoff?"}
  F -->|yes| G["NOAH"]
  F -->|no| H["market-rate"]
```

The chain, step by step:

1. **Affordable rent.** HUD's FY2024 Section 8 income limits give the 80% AMI (Low
   Income) limit for a 3-person household — a reference for a 2-bedroom unit. Lower
   AMI levels scale that limit. At 30% of income to rent, this fixes an affordable
   monthly rent (\$1,651/mo in the Maryland metros at 60% AMI).
2. **Rent to market value.** A gross rent multiplier (10×, the mid-point of the
   ~9–11× range for stabilized multifamily) converts affordable annual rent to an
   affordable *market* value per unit.
3. **Market to assessed value.** The parcels carry *assessed* value, which sits
   below market in both jurisdictions. The **assessment ratio** — assessed over
   sale price — is estimated directly from arms-length multifamily sales in the
   study parcels themselves (median 0.75 in Maryland from 1,586 sales, 0.93 in DC
   from 935), not borrowed from a statewide figure. Multiplying by it puts the
   cutoff on the same footing as the assessed values the buildings carry.

The AMI level is the one substantive lever, and three are stored so the label's
sensitivity is visible. **60% AMI is the default** — it is the income level LIHTC
units are targeted to, so NOAH is drawn at the same affordability line the
subsidized stock serves.

| Variant | AMI level | Maryland cutoff | DC cutoff |
|---|---|---|---|
| ami50 | 50% AMI | ~\$123k | ~\$153k |
| ami60 (default) | 60% AMI (LIHTC standard) | ~\$148k | ~\$184k |
| ami80 | 80% AMI | ~\$197k | ~\$245k |

This replaces an earlier per-state quantile cutoff, which drew the NOAH line at a
fixed fraction of each state's own value distribution. The quantile was
reproducible but arbitrary — "the cheapest 40%" is not an affordability standard —
and made NOAH mean something different in each state. The AMI-anchored cutoff is an
absolute, interpretable threshold: a NOAH building is one whose assessed value is
consistent with rents affordable at or below the target AMI. Assessed value remains
a proxy for rent (it lags market and is struck on staggered reassessment cycles),
so the label is only as sharp as the proxy and the GRM — which is why the AMI
variants and the measured assessment ratio are both carried explicitly.

### Parcel-scale environment

Each environmental measure is extracted over the building footprint using the same
rasters and the same canopy arithmetic as the areal pipeline, so a building's canopy
percent and a block group's canopy percent differ only in the polygon, not the
definition. An optional buffer extracts over a ring around the footprint to capture
the building's immediate green context rather than only its roof and pavement.

```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
  A["Building footprint"] --> B["Chesapeake 1 m<br/>-> canopy_pct"]
  A --> C["Landsat summer median<br/>-> mean_lst, mean_ndvi"]
  B --> E["Typed building<br/>+ environment table"]
  C --> E
```

A FEMA National Flood Hazard Layer overlay is coded (Special Flood Hazard Area
intersection) but not yet in the dashboard: the current NFHL pull is partial, so a
flood flag would undercount exposure. It is deferred until a complete flood layer is
loaded rather than shipped as an incomplete variable.

## Neighborhood covariates and the adjusted contrast

The unadjusted type contrast conditions on nothing, so any gap mixes the effect of
housing type with where each type sits. To separate the two, each building is joined
to its block group and a set of ACS 2022 5-year covariates — median household income,
poverty rate, share with a bachelor's degree or higher, Black and Hispanic
population shares, and population density. These enter an adjusted regression of the
environmental measure on housing type.

The estimand is a *conditional* exposure difference: the gap between NOAH (or
subsidized) and market-rate multifamily after accounting for observed structure and
neighborhood characteristics. It is not a causal effect. Unobserved selection —
why a building of a given type sits where it does — is not addressed.

```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
  A["Environmental measure<br/>(canopy, LST, ...)"] --> M["OLS, HC3 robust SEs"]
  B["Housing type dummies<br/>market-rate = baseline"] --> M
  C["Structure: age,<br/>log lot area, log units"] --> M
  D["ACS block group: income,<br/>poverty, education, race, density"] --> M
  E["Jurisdiction fixed effects"] --> M
  M --> G["Gap vs market-rate,<br/>per state + pooled"]
```

Modeling choices worth stating. The outcome stays in natural units (percentage
points, degrees) so a coefficient reads directly as a canopy or temperature gap;
continuous controls are standardized so their coefficients are comparable and the
intercept stays interpretable. Market-rate is the omitted baseline, so each
housing-type coefficient is that type's gap against comparable market-rate stock.
The model is fit **separately for Maryland and DC** — the raw gap has opposite signs
in the two, and a pooled fit would average that away — with a pooled specification
(jurisdiction fixed effects) shown alongside for reference.

## Reading the housing-type dashboard

Five views, all filterable by state, housing type, and environmental measure.

- **Type map.** A block-group choropleth of the chosen environmental measure, with
  buildings drawn as points colored by housing type over it. A legend names the
  three types. It answers where each type sits relative to the surrounding
  environmental gradient.
- **Type choropleth.** Block-group counts of units of the selected housing types,
  so the concentration of NOAH or market-rate stock reads at the areal scale the
  first dashboard uses.
- **Type contrast.** The median environmental measure for each type, Maryland and DC
  shown separately. These are raw differences, no controls.
- **Value and environment.** Value per unit against the environmental measure, with
  the NOAH cutoff drawn as a horizontal line, so the affordability gradient and the
  threshold's position are both visible.
- **Adjusted gaps.** The regression gaps against market-rate, unadjusted (hollow)
  and controls-adjusted (solid), with 95% confidence intervals, per state.

The headline value box reports the raw **difference in medians** between NOAH and
market-rate on the chosen measure — a positive canopy value means NOAH sits at
higher canopy, a positive temperature value means it sits hotter. It is a
descriptive difference, not a correlation or an adjusted estimate; the Adjusted gaps
tab is where controls enter.

## What the contrast shows, and a caution

The NOAH-versus-market difference is **not the same sign in the two jurisdictions**,
which is the first thing the dashboard makes visible and the strongest argument
against reading a single pooled number.

| State | Type | Median canopy % | Median summer LST (°C) |
|---|---|---|---|
| DC | NOAH | 15.1 | 40.2 |
| DC | Market-rate | 5.5 | 41.1 |
| MD | NOAH | 20.9 | 40.7 |
| MD | Market-rate | 21.9 | 40.1 |

In DC, NOAH buildings sit at markedly higher canopy and lower summer surface
temperature than market-rate multifamily. In Maryland the two are close to
indistinguishable. The DC pattern is consistent with location: the District's
low-value walk-up apartments, which the value proxy labels NOAH, sit more in leafier
residential neighborhoods, while market-rate multifamily concentrates in the dense,
hot, low-canopy core. This is an interpretation, not a finding — the comparison
conditions on nothing, and the same value proxy that defines NOAH is correlated with
the neighborhoods a building sits in. Read the contrast as a description of where
these types are, and treat the cross-state reversal as a reason to model the two
jurisdictions separately rather than pool them.

The adjusted contrast, with NOAH defined at 60% AMI, points the same direction in
both states: NOAH multifamily sits in greener, cooler locations than comparable
market-rate stock. For tree canopy the NOAH advantage is positive everywhere and
strengthens under controls in Maryland — from +4.2 pp raw to +7.4 pp adjusted (95%
CI +5.2 to +9.7) — while in DC the large raw gap of +7.2 pp *attenuates* to +2.1 pp
(+0.4 to +3.8) once neighborhood is held constant, so most of the District's raw gap
is composition but a conditional gap remains. Summer surface temperature agrees:
adjusted, NOAH buildings are about 1.0 °C cooler than market-rate in Maryland (−1.0,
CI −1.3 to −0.7) and 0.5 °C cooler in DC (−0.5, −0.7 to −0.3). The consistency of
sign is more credible than the earlier quantile labeling produced, because NOAH now
means a fixed affordability standard rather than "the locally cheaper stock." None of
it is causal: the comparison conditions on observed characteristics only, and
unobserved selection into where each housing type is built still sits behind the
affordability proxy.

---

*The complete documentation follows.*

{{< include references/data_and_methods.md >}}
